item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image sourced to here, which doesn't state any specific license. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked both review earlier and these notes. More SCPs need that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reason this one really hit me is that I understood the words being presented, but not in the order they were in. The concept of intestines snaking through multiple people and into holes is so bizarre and alien that despite being described understandably, I was unable to grasp what I was reading (at first). Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is unrelated to the actual article, but I feel like the letter to General Electric would make a good copypasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that's intentional. Consider that SCP-1193 does not appear to understand the function or reasonable dimensions of ovens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd be super curious to know what happens when SCP-1877-01 & -02 undergo surgery. The whole time I was reading I was wondering "why don't they just surgically sever the extracorporeal intestines?" Unless it should be assumed that surgery is impossible due to the subjects' inability to leave their houses. But still, it was very interesting, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A particularly interesting detail is that the serial number PD968DPBB corresponds to an actual GE oven, but SCP-1877-03 clearly thinks it's a dishwasher. I could imagine that SCP-1877-03 is confused about dishwashers and ovens (the related SCP-1193 certainly has a different understanding of appliances from our own), but the bit that really has me scratching my head is that Foundation personnel reported finding the note in a dishwasher. I took the note left in the dishwasher to imply that SCP-1877-03 left it there on his way through it, but that would imply that it really was a dishwasher. Did the recovery team also view an oven as a dishwasher? Perhaps a minor oversight in a brilliantly understated joke? This SCP is weird enough that it's hard to really tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew it! I feel so good, because before reading your author description I had already gotten the sense that this seems like it takes place in the same space as the Buried Giant. I don't usually post theories, so I never actually said it, but man I feel so good having caught that vibe from this article. I didn't even know it was the same author.

Sorry, but this is the first time I've had a theory of mine confirmed, and it felt amazing. I get it if no one cares.

That being said, A+ article, man. Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Superficially similar to 1193, yes, but I find this much more mind boggling and enjoyable, in a very creepy way. Absolutely loved this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, but the description could use a summary. This is a technical document after all. While it's fairly clear that the anomalous disease is the extra corporeal intestine, it doesn't actually say that anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to know more about the earth intestines :( +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and the same may happen to 01.. but the real question is what will happen to 02 when both ends of the intestines get tighter and tighter?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Woo! +100! (I now have only two SCPs — 1427 and 1272 — which are under 100.)

The Guts of the Earth are creeping in me
The world unbirth prolongs infinitely

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just wondering why no one has discovered this yet. I mean, the intestines do reach some rather public places, such as a metro tunnel. Surely those workers who do maintenance work in the tunnels would have spotted those intestines by now?

Edit:

Any publicly-accessible infrastructure containing any length of extracorporeal intestine is to be rotated out of service if practicable, or serviced only by Foundation-approved personnel if impractical.

Okay, I see. Still though, someone sharp enough might have spotted the intestines from the trains? Unless they somehow managed to hide it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's extremely similar to 1193, and so I'm glad there won't be another one, but I still adored this one. Horrendously weird and creepy (more of the latter than former than 1193, so I think I prefer 1193) and, of course, flawlessly written. Good job, ophite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fridge horror as far as I see it...
The Adelaide individual was sucked into the dishwasher. That in itself is the scariest part to me. Trying to imagine it. How does he contort?

This is some Junji Ito-type, back-of-the-mind shit. Very good work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost want to e-mail General Electric a copy of the letter 'in good faith' just to see what kind of response I get. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This type of explanation, while some might consider tedious, is what I now realize I have been craving. I will say that if something similar accompanied all SCP articles, my -1 votes would shrink astronomically. I am one reader who looks for the background explanations, the aptly stated Director's commentary. To me it adds the delicious seasoning that can make an otherwise obtuse piece of writing really sparkle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this article, and it should go without saying that I really like Ophite's commentary. I find that kind of breakdown both incredibly enjoyable to read and extremely instructive. It's the sort of thing that fills me with naked envy, in a good way. Keep on keepin' on, duder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially liked this a lot, just a neat concept I hadn't seen before. Then there was a power outage that lasted a few days, setting me scrambling to find outlets anywhere I could to charge my phone. It occurred to me that I was sort of leashed to the outlet via my charger cord, and I connected that to this article, and…eurgh. Now it creeps me the fuck out and I like it even more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … GE haven't sold dishwashers in Australia in my lifetime. This is as off-putting as reading about a modern-day agent driving his new De Dion-Bouton to work. But the tweak in the suspension of disbelief somehow fits the tone of the whole thing—I'm not sure if it makes me like the article more or less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A tale is a whole different beast and wouldn't be subject to the same diminishing returns; especially if it focuses more on explaining the underpinning dream-logic and exploring the internal rules that governs these things.

I'm looking forward to reading that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. There won't be a third instance. (Though there's a tale in the hopper that goes over the connection between the two.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the caption, I had an idea of what this was and who wrote it.
The concept is good, solid body-horror with shades of Kronenberg machine/flesh blending.
The execution is also solid, good pacing with a good step effect to the reveals.

But it is just so similar to the arm that I can't appreciate it as much as I otherwise would. I still upvoted, but I don't think I would for a third instance of this style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would a capsule get past that sharp turn?
It seems to me that it would, but I have zero actual knowledge of the subject.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Almost silly enough to make me switch to a downvote

I know you were using hyperbole, Anaxagoras, and because of that, this isn't directed at you.

But I need to say that no one is allowed to downvote things based on authorial actions, ever. That is malicious downvoting and will result in a moderative action. (Anyone can, of course, simply remove their vote.)

Everyone else, please do not respond to this post, as it's regarding a conversation that the participants have already agreed to end. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author commentaries are the last thing we need to discourage, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, it is?

That…

Stop fucking with my brain! >:|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well damn, now I just feel inadequate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure. No big deal — already forgotten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You phrased that politely. Look, again, I'm sorry. This is a good article, it really is. You were just in the wrong place at the wrong time. You couldn't have known I was like this. Just, let's end this conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally get that. If it's any consolation, I think you're a great writer and have nothing to be anxious about, which is why I was absolutely not going to be a dick about your objection.

You just do naturally what I'm forced to do systematically, which means that this is probably both unhelpful to you and a bit of a provocation. Which means, unfortunately, that it's just a difference of opinion, and I can't do anything else to help you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Look, I'm sorry. You've fallen into a trap you could not possibly have predicted.

I have crippling self-esteem issues that I make up for by being a megalomaniac at times. Chiefly, I believe that I am the most intelligent human being ever to have lived. This is, of course, false, but I can't ever let myself think that, or else I wouldn't be me.

You have written something that demonstrates that you put more thought into your creative process than I do into mine. Therefore, you are trying to kill me. Again, ridiculous, but a part of me does feel that way. To clarify, through writing this, it demonstrates a form of intelligence that I can not easily dismiss as lesser, because I do not use my intelligence in a formal way in my writing. Though I can't speak for your mathematical knowledge or what have you, I must be the smartest in all domains to be the smartest overall. Since I do not not use my intelligence formally in writing, I cannot prove that I am smarter than you, so I might not be me. Which I think is the same as being dead. Look, I know this isn't rational, but it's the only reasoning I've got.

Again, this is not your fault (or perhaps it is, if that foolishness on your part gives me a way out of the spiral of my own damned thoughts). You just caught me at a bad time (overslept, missed a class, hadn't had breakfast), with something that really does seem written in an "I'm so smart" kind of way. You could actually treat my distress as a very twisted sort of compliment, if you were so inclined, because I wouldn't be so upset if you had written something easier to dismiss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this. I think we could always use more SCPs like this, and while I'm going to refrain from using the above as a model wholesale, there are definitely lessons to be learnt from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't imagine why you're "really pissed off." I can't imagine what you think is an "illegitimate use" for terms you obviously understand. I certainly can't imagine what I could do about either, other than not have written this. Charitably, I suspect your problem is that the vocabulary of literary criticism was invented by pretentious dicks, and all of it strongly carries that stink.

Unfortunately, as far as I'm aware, there's no alternative vocabulary which sounds less dickish.

If you have any particular suggestions for how to make this more helpful or less arrogant, I'm willing to take them. If you just think I'm a dick, then, well, I suppose there's nothing I can do about that, and don't know why you felt the need to tell me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps, yes. I haven't had any food yet today, which may be influencing me adversely. Nonetheless, this really pissed me off with 1193, and it's happening again here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about we all take some time to unrustle our jimmies, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the similarities are extremely strong. I prefer this implementation of the idea, but no matter. It doesn't ruin my experience of the article. It ruins my experience of you. Because the way it reads is very much "hurf durf look at how brilliant I am." Just like your explanation for 1193 was. Truth be told, I recalled that you were the same author as that, and was dreading you replicating that explanation here.

All your explanation of these little tricks does is make me doubt your veracity, and lessen the accomplishment of writing this. I'm sorry, this is rude of me. It's just that terms like "negative space", "intentional understatement", "technique", "category-violating world", and a number of others have zero legitimate use other than trying to show off. Plus, you continue your preposterous theory that it is the notion that we don't understand things about the world that is the most horrific. Again, sorry. But reading your post makes me very angry. Good article, don't ruin it with this post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Countering Anaxagoras, I find this commentary to be incredibly insightful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, what am I supposed to do?

The process is ludicrously pretentious, but, I mean, it's also actually what I did. It's always ugly and eye-rolly when someone breaks down a creative process into digestible bits for other peoples' consumption, and it always sounds like ridiculous lit-crit bullshit. Because of course that's what it is.

While writing this, when I had a major tonal problem (like "how do I convey the heart of the weird thing that's happening"), I solved it by going back to the formal structure and applying something I'd done before. Which isn't to say "hurf durf look at how brilliant I am" — in fact, I think this basically isn't a huge innovation. From the perspective of the formal structure, this is exactly the same SCP as SCP-1193: containment procedures which act like a teaser, followed by an understatement and more teasing, followed by rapidly unspooling high weirdness, followed by the horror punch, then a transition into a primary document you're meant to distrust which delivers the punch-line.

That structure isn't magic. It can be replicated. But the commentary isn't for everyone, and might (as it obviously did for you) ruin the experience of reading it. It's just, hopefully, a useful explanation of how to replicate the process that produced this particular SCP, or how to evaluate 'what might be a good idea for a SCP,' or at least how to solve potential tonal and flow problems when they come up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is silly. Almost silly enough to make me switch to a downvote, but the letter is well-written enough that I can't quite do that in good conscience. Still, this reads as ludicrously pretentious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Director's Commentary
Director's Commentary

Special Containment Procedures: Due to the impossibility of transport, each instance of SCP-1877 is contained in situ at the subject's origin point. When practicable, the Foundation shall secure ownership of the origin point, soundproof the subject's living quarters, and install inward-facing locks on any interior doorways adjacent to the subject.

The actual procedures aren't so important to this SCP. In this case, however, the horror is in what's happening to the subjects, not what the subjects might do to the outside world. Which is why the procedures are what they are: they're establishing an entirely impersonal mood. Whoever is containing these people does not care about their fate.

By this point, we are clear that SCP is humanoid, immobile, and might try to escape.

Foundation medical staff are to review subjects' condition on a 48-hour cycle, measuring any visible extracorporeal intestine. Any intestinal retraction of over 10mm in a single 48-hour period is to be immediately reported to Research Director ████.

Here, the containment procedure implies the nature of the SCP. Unless you have some other reason not to do this (e.g., confusion about the SCP's nature is part of your point), there should probably be something that does this in your procedures — a section which describes it in negative space.

Any publicly-accessible infrastructure containing any length of extracorporeal intestine is to be rotated out of service if practicable, or serviced only by Foundation-approved personnel if impractical. Endoscopic discovery of any additional extracorporeal intestine is to be reported immediately to the Research Director.

And then I confound the expectations I've just delivered. What is 'extracorporeal intestine,' and what is it doing in our pipes?

SCP-1877 is an anomalous gastrointestinal disorder affecting a number of geographically disparate individuals, designated SCP-1877-01 through SCP-1877-03.

"Anomalous gastrointestinal disorder" is an intentional understatement, a technique I tend to use early in the description, to set expectations low. You can see something similar in SCP-1193, when I call the entity 'a human arm of unusual length,' without specifying how unusual the length is.

In addition to their abnormally long and continuous gastrointestinal tract, subjects exhibit severe neuropsychiatric symptoms including left hemispatial neglect and mild aphasia. While subjects' immobility renders most medical imaging impracticable, preliminary EEG results demonstrate unusually high levels of electrical activity in patients' right parietal lobes.

This is just a tease which delays the punch. Aphasia also explains why the letter which occurs later in the article is so weird and stilted.

To date, over 30 km of continuous intestine has been endoscopically mapped. Subjects' intestinal tracts have been observed to intersect with numerous manmade artifacts, including pilot lights, electrical conduits, lightbulb sockets, telephone receivers, water mains, sewage pipes, and subway tunnels. 31 hours after entering SCP-1877-01's digestive tract, objects which have entered that tract can be observed passing through SCP-1877-02's entry stoma and exiting 30 minutes thereafter. Presumably, they then travel to SCP-1877-03, and are passed to additional individuals or excreted.

Insofar as SCPs are something other than pure horror, they're about category confusion: something is not what it ought to be. What categories am I confusing here? The human body and the world of material artifacts. Our poor subjects have their bodies spread out through the world of manufactured goods.

We do not know why, but there is something horrible running through our pipes and wires and sewers.

4cm beyond the pyloric valve, SCP-1877-01's duodenum turns outward through a painless stoma in his abdominal wall, extends across subject's living room, and enters a light fixture located in the ceiling of his apartment. Architectural and endoscopic investigation of his gastrointestinal tract seems to indicate that it then descends through the wall of his apartment, enters a water line, diverts into the Washington DC metro system, and then takes a sharp southward turn which renders endoscopy impracticable without severe injury to the patient.

Here, the category confusion continues. Note the phrase "architectural and endoscopic."

I've tried to give a vivid image of the first subject's apartment: his guts are strung across his living room, and enter his light fixture. Note, by the way, that poor SCP-1877-01 is the guy with the short intestine. He's not being hauled sideways into his dishwasher: not long from now, he'll be hauled into the air, dangling by his guts from a lightbulb socket.

The subject's gastrointestinal tract then resumes in Busan, Korea, exiting through a pilot light in SCP-1877-02's oven, traversing the kitchen and bathroom, and entering through a painless stoma in the second subject's abdominal wall. After 1.4m of superficially normal small intestine, a second length of jejunum exits through a second stoma in the anterior abdominal wall, descends into the subject's bathroom sink, and passes 3.7km through the Busan sewer system.

This is slightly repetitive, but the exposition is necessary: I need more than one subject, and I need to prove that he's far away. Busan is a good place for that.

Endoscopic investigation has demonstrated that SCP-1877-02's upper digestive tract is no longer continuous with the lower digestive tract now occupying his body.

The guts of the earth are, in a sense, parasitic. This is not SCP-1877-02's intestine.

A third individual, designated SCP-1877-03, is presumed to exist but has not been found. Presuming that the southward bearing of SCP-1877-02's small intestine is continuous, the uncontained third subject is hypothesized to live somewhere within 30km of Adelaide, Australia.

And now I have introduced you to the letter-writer.

At present, subjects' condition is unstable. Beginning in 2007, both known subjects' extracorporeal intestines began to retract into architectural features of their apartments. At recovery, SCP-1877-01 presented with 24m of slack extracorporeal intestine, permitting him limited mobility within his quarters. At present, SCP-1877-01 only possesses 8m of taut extracorporeal intestine, confining him to a single room of his apartment. SCP-1877-02's retraction has been less severe; at recovery, subject presented with 41m of slack intestine. Postrecovery retraction has reduced his intestinal slack to 34m.

This is horror, not category confusion. And now that the description's done, we've set up the confused categories and introduced the horror of being hauled around by your intestines. Now I need to deliver the punch line. This is a problem.

So, what's the problem with that? Well, mostly, that this article is bearing an extraordinarily high level of WTF-load. I can't deliver more WTF in the punch line without breaking suspension of disbelief. But more on that later.

On December 5th, 2012, a Foundation recovery team entered an apartment in Adelaide hypothesized to be the origin point of SCP-1877-03. No anomalous features were detected; however, a handwritten letter was found in the apartment's dishwasher. That letter is transcribed below:

Remember how our subjects are brain-damaged wrecks, in addition to having their intestines stretched everywhere? That's not just random extra symptoms. If I need to deliver maximum WTF in a single article, I need to give the audience a license to disbelieve. You can see me using this device in both SCP-1348 and SCP-1193: in SCP-1348, audio interviews with religious fanatics and tractable, brain-damaged amnesiacs give you permission to disbelieve; in SCP-1193, the phone call with the buried giant is so bizarre that you can't be sure whether you're understanding it correctly.

In both cases, this (a) gives you a break from the depersonalization of the SCP, and (b) provides you with a narrator you are only meant to partially believe. This letter does the same.

Dear General Electric,

Why General Electric? The other category-conflict I'm setting up is between the horrible and the mundane. I don't want to imply that the horrible is infiltrating the mundane. I want to imply that the horrible and mundane are drawn from the same wellspring.

I have long appreciated your products. But now I draw close to the socket and there are questions.

The tone, here, is meant to fall squarely between 'prayer' and 'tech support letter.' Note how every instance of 'socket' could probably be replaced with the word 'death.'

For instance what is the problem? I think the problem is that there is a bad disease. My stomach hurts. And I own a General Electric dishwasher, model PD968DPBB, and it does not even help me.

Here, we have the subjective viewpoint of one of the victims, and an apparent non-sequitur about his dishwasher. Why does he expect it to help his stomach ache? We don't know, and can't know.

Everything growls and boils and whips. There is a stink.

Something is happening here which isn't happening in the other sites. What is 'growling and boiling and whipping?' Obviously, not 'everything,' literally. What is the stink coming from?

I have called my cousin in America and told him that there were difficulties with sickness.

Note the vague intertextual link to SCP-1193.

He told me to call you, because you are where the problems come from, that there is an electrical wave which comes from my dishwasher and goes to my guts.

This is meant to sound vaguely schizophrenic. 'Stilted' isn't enough. I need to establish that some of the subject's perceptions are unreliable. But he is already in an incredibly bizarre situation. How could you possibly know which of his perceptions are unreliable?

Also what will happen? The problem is now very bad. I have to stand so close to your socket that I can hear the electrical wave. Like whalesong. I will have to go in soon. My guts will make me. I know that General Electric cannot make it stop.

We have established that he does not blame General Electric, and that the 'electrical wave' which causes his disease is not wholly bad. He thinks it's like whalesong. But, like the growling and boiling and whipping, and the stink, this is something which is not happening to the other subjects.

But there needs to be an answer to the mystery of the appliances. The answer should come in the manual which goes with the socket. So that there is less anxiety when we all have to go into our appliances and live in the guts of the earth, as all of us someday shall do.

Here, the subject is clearly talking about death, which he thinks occurs as a result of appliances. But why does he think everyone enters their appliances and lives in the guts of the earth? There are broader implications here, but he takes them as a given, and blows right past.

Tomorrow I think I will have to go in. When it ends, will I live alone in the names of appliances? How does one traverse the guts of the earth so that there is no problem? Is this a sickness that shoots in an electrical wave from all dishwashers, or just mine?

The subject is close to accepting his fate. But even though he appears to understand it, or have viable theories about it, what do those theories entail? What does it mean to 'live alone in the names of appliances?', as he fears. As the author, I have a vague idea about what that might entail, but this is pretty close to word salad, even to me. But I have hopefully, by this point, delivered enough that the reader is attempting to form theories about word this word salad.

Though the letter appears never to have been sent, a review of telephone logs indicates that a call was placed to the apartment's telephone from General Electric's headquarters in Fairfield, CT. The precise origin point of that call, the subject matter of the conversation which followed, and the individual responsible are still undetermined as of █/██/████.

And here, finally, we have the conclusion to the punchline. The Foundation does not understand what is going on here. They just contain it. But somewhere, in an office in Fairfield, CT, someone has received an unsent letter, and provided answers to our poor subject. That person lives in the mundane world, not in the bizarre, category-violating world of the Foundation.

In other words, we are misunderstanding something terribly. Something which people in the outside world understand well. And, nope, you don't get any more information on what it is: this is the end of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah sorry about the distortion. I can't do australian :p.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A touch too jagged with the distortion.

But yeah, this one definitely calls for a fan audio. Now, who can do an Australian accent convincingly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do fan audio.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been a long wait, but you didn't disappoint. Welcome back sir, and well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being pulled around by my intestines sounds like the worst thing ever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Endless intestines? I love it. Way better than hurrrr its a closet what go on furever or hurrrr drawers what be infinity

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only issue with this is that it is extremely difficult to map more that a few meters of intestine due to the pressure from the air needed to insufflate the intestine to see, as well as the amount of tubing/power to run the endoscopy machinery. Capsule endoscopy would have been more efficient.

However, this is so well written that I have to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At General Electric, there is apparently someone who understands that dishwashers make an electric wave which attracts the guts

Plot twist: it's Jack Donaghy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Without implying a common GOI or proposing any overt connection between the two (other than the "cousin in America"), this is actually intended to be the same phenomenon that's responsible for the Buried Giant. I have a tale in the hopper about… not the connection, necessarily, but about the phenomenon in general.

And, yes, the intestines are hanging out for everyone to see, though almost their entire length is hidden inside subway tunnels, electrical conduits, sewer pipes, and similar structures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this because it's a cross between the monster arm, the lady with the Nazi bunker in her nose, and the Human Centipede, and yet I upvoted because the writing is just so damned compelling. Probably also for the phrase "extracorporeal intestine". Am I reading this correctly that their intestines are actually just hanging around the world, for everyone to see? I had initially anticipated that it was just a spatial anomaly linking them together, along with the architectural bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can explain it:

The theme of this SCP is that the mundane is horrible. Underneath the most trivial things in our lives — dishwashers, subways, light fixtures — there is a pulsing, world-spanning web of living intestines. The existence of that horror is not the responsibility of some external force. It is not an imposition on mundanity.

Rather, the horror comes from precisely the same source as the mundane.

At General Electric, there is apparently someone who understands that dishwashers make an electric wave which attracts the guts — whatever that means. And he was polite enough to respond. Perhaps he even explained. But we will never know what he said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We need more line-by-lines like this. It's just as useful for people to know what they're doing right as what they're doing wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is made like twice as good by the fact that you chose a real, well-known company rather than making one up. I can't put my finger on it, but General Electric is perfect for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely weird - endless intestines that travel across the world? - but the interview and the body horror pull it off spectacularily. +1

Although why a dishwasher causing the effects? I'm not sure I understand that bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow. uh, what? I kind of understand this…not really…upvote. it's weird enough.
